verdict: PASS - reading.md is a faithful, well-scoped condensation of topic_corpus.md for 6.3. Six required sections present in order (Overview, Key Concepts, Worked Example, In Practice, Key Takeaways, References) with no YAML frontmatter -- first non-empty line is the H1 '# Special Methods & Dataclasses'. reading.meta.json exists and parses with topic_id, title, estimated_read_minutes, word_count, generated_at, embeds_diagram, citations_used, sections. Independent whitespace-token count of the raw file (via offset/head_limit binary search: match found at offset=1953 giving a 1954th token, no match at offset=1954) came to 1954 tokens -- close to but not identical to the sidecar's self-reported word_count=1922 (a ~1.7% gap plausibly from how markdown/code-fence punctuation tokenizes under raw \S+ splitting vs the sidecar's word-counter); both figures sit comfortably inside the deep-profile target_word_range [1800, 2200], so the length budget passes either way. Reading-aloud time at 230 wpm is ~8.3-8.5 min for both counts, inside the range implied by that word band (~7.8-9.6 min); the sidecar's estimated_read_minutes=10 uses a slower/different display formula -- a cosmetic discrepancy, not a length-budget fail, the same pattern already noted in the 6.1 and 6.2 reports. Both required primary resources are cited: [1] docs.python.org/3/reference/datamodel.html appears in Key Concepts (special methods/data model, object's default repr, __repr__/__str__ fallback, __eq__ identity fallback and and-chained equality, module objects), Worked Example, and Implementation steps -- well over a dozen times; [2] docs.python.org/3/library/dataclasses.html is cited repeatedly across the @dataclass subsection, the Worked Example dataclass version, and In Practice (DTO/record framing, order=True); [3] realpython.com/python-repr-vs-str is cited alongside [1] wherever repr/str convention is discussed. The References section lists exactly [1][2][3] with matching titles/URLs, no orphan inline markers, no orphan bibliography entries. No forward references: read content/curriculum/sequence.index.json directly and confirmed 1.1-1.4 and 6.1-6.2 have populated concepts_introduced (status corpus_drafted), while 2.1 through 5.3 all have empty concepts_introduced arrays (status scaffolded, not yet built) -- 6.3 draws only on 6.1/6.2 vocabulary (Task/RegulatoryTask/Person, super(), isinstance(), is, MRO) plus its own new material. Specifically verified zero if/elif/else statements or ternary expressions in code: grepped '\bif\b|\belif\b|\belse\b' and every hit (lines 23, 52, 121, 129) is the English conditional/idiomatic word used in prose ('if other isn't a Task at all, the expression returns False...', 'as if it lived in the same file', 'if == compares values') describing behaviour in plain language, never a Python if/elif/else or ternary construct inside a code fence -- the __eq__ examples throughout use only the and-chained boolean expression starting with isinstance(), matching the corpus's purged style. Grepped every function/method signature ('def \\w+\\([^)]*=') for default parameter values: zero matches -- every def in every code block (hand-written __init__/__repr__/__str__/__eq__, mark_complete, RegulatoryTask.__init__, greet/have_birthday) takes only required positional parameters. The only default-value assignments found ('done: bool = False', repeated in each @dataclass field-declaration block) all sit inside @dataclass class bodies as field declarations, exactly as required.

checks.name: Format discipline
checks.detail: First non-empty line is '# Special Methods & Dataclasses' -- no YAML frontmatter block. reading.meta.json exists and parses, containing topic_id, title, estimated_read_minutes, word_count, generated_at, embeds_diagram, citations_used, sections. Exactly six '##' headings appear in the required order: Overview, Key Concepts, Worked Example, In Practice, Key Takeaways, References -- none added, renamed, dropped, or reordered.

checks.name: Length budget
checks.detail: Independent whitespace-token count via Grep offset/head_limit binary search converged on 1954 tokens (match at offset=1953, no match at offset=1954). Sidecar self-reports word_count=1922. Both values fall inside the deep-profile target_word_range [1800, 2200]. Reading-aloud estimate at 230 wpm is ~8.3-8.5 minutes for either count, inside the range implied by that word band (~7.8-9.6 min). The sidecar's estimated_read_minutes=10 does not match 1922/230 exactly, but this is a display-formula discrepancy already flagged as non-blocking in the 6.1/6.2 reports, not a length-budget fail.

checks.name: Citations
checks.detail: [1] (docs.python.org/3/reference/datamodel.html, primary) is cited throughout Key Concepts (special methods/data model, object's default __repr__, __repr__/__str__ convention and fallback rule, __eq__ identity fallback and and-chained equality, module objects/namespaces), the Worked Example commentary, and Implementation steps 2-4 -- well above the 'at least once' bar. [2] (docs.python.org/3/library/dataclasses.html, primary) is cited across the entire @dataclass subsection (field generation, ordering rule, frozen/order arguments, RegulatoryTask-inherits-from-dataclass note), the dataclass Worked Example, and In Practice's DTO/order=True bullets. [3] (realpython.com/python-repr-vs-str) is cited alongside [1] wherever the repr/str convention and fallback are discussed. References section lists exactly [1], [2], [3] with matching titles/URLs; no orphan inline markers and no orphan bibliography entries either direction.

checks.name: No forward references
checks.detail: Read content/curriculum/sequence.index.json directly: 6.3 lists prerequisites 6.1 and 6.2, both status='corpus_drafted' with populated concepts_introduced; 1.1-1.4 are also status='corpus_drafted'/populated. Topics 2.1 Conditionals, 2.2 Loops, 3.1 Functions, 3.2 Functional Constructs, 3.3 Modules/Packaging, 4.1-4.2 Lists/Tuples, and 5.1-5.3 Sets/Dictionaries/Iterators are all status='scaffolded' with empty concepts_introduced arrays -- confirmed not yet built -- and the reading draws only on 6.1/6.2 material (Task/RegulatoryTask/Person, super(), isinstance(), is, MRO) plus its own new content. Specifically grepped '\\bif\\b|\\belif\\b|\\belse\\b': the 4 hits (lines 23, 52, 121, 129) are all the English conditional/idiomatic word in prose ('if other isn't a Task at all, the expression returns False immediately', 'as if it had been defined locally' / 'as if it lived in the same file', 'if == compares values') -- none are Python if/elif/else statements or ternary expressions inside a code fence; the corpus's and-chained isinstance()-first __eq__ pattern is preserved verbatim in every __eq__ example (hand-written Task, RegulatoryTask extending via super().__eq__(), and the dataclass-generated equivalent described in prose). Also grepped 'def \\w+\\([^)]*=' for default parameter values in plain signatures: zero matches -- every __init__/__repr__/__str__/__eq__/mark_complete/greet/have_birthday def takes only required positional parameters. All 'done: bool = False'-style defaults appear exclusively as @dataclass field declarations inside @dataclass class bodies, never in a plain method or function signature.

checks.name: Faithfulness to corpus
checks.detail: Sampled 12 assertions spanning all sections against topic_corpus.md: the print(t1)-shows-a-memory-address / t1==t2-is-False motivating example (corpus Introduction/4), the dunder-method definition and __init__-as-implicit-call framing (5.1), object's unhelpful default repr and the '__repr__ should look like the code that recreates the object' convention with !r inside f-strings (5.2), the object.__str__-calls-self.__repr__() fallback rule (5.2), == defaulting to identity comparison inherited from object (5.3), the and-chained isinstance-first __eq__ body and its left-to-right short-circuit evaluation (5.3), RegulatoryTask needing its own __eq__ via super().__eq__(other) to cover regulation_code (5.3), @dataclass generating __init__/__repr__/__eq__ from field declarations (5.4), the defaulted-field-must-follow-non-defaulted-field ordering rule (5.4), @dataclass not generating __str__ and print() falling back to __repr__ (5.4), frozen=True/order=True as optional decorator arguments (5.4), and the module/import/package mechanics including the module-object-as-namespace-dictionary trace (5.5) -- all traced verbatim or as faithful paraphrase, no new facts introduced. One unsupported assertion found, consistent with the pattern already flagged in the 6.1 and 6.2 reports: the Overview's closing italic sentence ('This contributes to A3 -- Python Foundations Diagnostic (due W8)...') has no basis in topic_corpus.md (confirmed via direct grep for 'A3'/'Diagnostic'/'W8' -- zero matches in the corpus); this is LMS/assessment scheduling metadata, not a taught concept, and 1 unsupported assertion is well under the >2 fail threshold.

checks.name: Tone fit
checks.detail: Zero hedging phrases found (searched 'generally considered', 'many experts believe', 'various studies', 'arguably', 'tends to', 'typically believed', 'studies show' and variants -- none present; threshold <3). Concrete worked code (Task's hand-written __repr__/__str__/__eq__, RegulatoryTask's super().__eq__ override, and the @dataclass field declarations) appears directly inside Key Concepts, not deferred solely to the Worked Example section, and every bolded term (special/dunder method, __repr__, __str__, __eq__, field, frozen, order, module, package) is defined in plain language at first use. Most paragraphs run 3-6 sentences; the __eq__ short-circuit paragraph and the @dataclass ordering-rule paragraph run longer and denser, but this mirrors the corpus's own necessarily precise mechanics-tracing style (same allowance already made for 6.1/6.2) rather than academic hedging or jargon-dumping.
